problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem A (continuous) asked teams to model the relationship between a cyclist's position on a time-trial course and the power they should apply, accounting for rider type/gender, course geometry, weather/environment, and to extend to a team time trial. This O-award paper (Team 2210307) frames it as: evaluate rider ability from a power-duration curve, quantify external resistances, then solve a constrained optimization that distributes power per course segment to minimize total finish time. Its title is "Power Planning Model: Magic Weapon for Cyclists."
models_used: Three-parameter critical power (3P-CP) model fitted to mean-maximal-power (MMP) vs duration scatter, yielding the rider-ability triple {Pmax, CP, W'} via the relation t = W'/(P-CP) + W'/(CP-Pmax)
Exercise intensity domain division (moderate/heavy/severe/extreme) per Burnley & Jones (2007) to justify which part of the power-duration curve is physically valid
Rider Ability Evaluation Model: 6-indicator profile {height h, mass m, Pmax (explosive), W' (stamina), CP (oxygen uptake), bike-control BC = height/(2.5*weight)} visualized as a hexagonal radar chart

External Factors Analysis Model: physics of resistances — rolling resistance Fr = kr*F; aerodynamic drag Fw = 1/2*cd*S*rho*v^2 with wind-relative speed vr = v - vw*cos(theta) and signed drag; ideal-gas air density rho = pM/(RT); barometric/altitude conversion; Stevenson formula for gender-specific windward area S

Per-segment dynamics (Newtonian force balance) for four segment types — flat, uphill, downhill, sharp corner (corner adds centripetal force Fc = m*v^2/r) — giving P as a function of v and slope/curvature

Power Overall Planning Model: multivariate nonlinear program, minimize t = sum(d_i / v_i) over per-segment power P_i, constrained by MMP(t) ceilings (P_i < 1.15*MMP), NP <= MMP, CP <= MMP, plus AP and NP (4th-power normalized power) definitions
Solved with gradient-descent / scipy.optimize.minimize using SLSQP (benchmarked against Nelder-Mead, Powell, CG, BFGS, Newton-CG, L-BFGS-B), with multi-start looping to approximate a global optimum

Sensitivity analysis via subset-simulation / importance-sampling idea: sensitivity expressed as partial derivative of conditional failure probability w.r.t. each variable (temperature, pressure, altitude, wind resistance), reporting COV and failure-probability/error rate

Team Time Trial extension: augmented NLP with strategy variables a, b, c (lead-swap fractions and leader power multiplier P_leader = c*P_ITT, c~130%), dropping aerodynamic-drag term for drafting (non-lead) riders
key_techniques: Decomposing a vague 'position-to-power' mapping into a clean two-stage pipeline: (1) rider physiology fit, (2) course physics + optimization — making an open-ended problem tractable

Choosing the 3P-CP model specifically because cited literature (Leo et al. 2022) shows it best matches the severe/extreme intensity domains that dominate race-relevant power, i.e. justifying model choice with domain evidence rather than convenience
Reducing rider heterogeneity to three fitted parameters {Pmax, CP, W'} that are simultaneously the fit outputs AND the optimization constraints — a tight, reusable coupling
Using Normalized Power (4th-root of power^4 time-average) to capture the physiological cost of variable effort, then encoding NP <= MMP as an optimization constraint to forbid unsustainable plans
Converting hard-to-measure air pressure into elevation via the barometric formula so course data (which lists altitude) can feed the air-density model
Providing a 'simplified 3-test protocol' (5s Pmax, 3-min CP from last-30s average, 5-min W' test) as a practical fallback when full power-curve data is unavailable — shows real-world deployability
Benchmarking multiple optimizer backends and selecting SLSQP on speed/time-complexity grounds, with explicit multi-start to mitigate local minima

Reframing sensitivity as failure probability per perturbed variable, producing a crisp sensitive/insensitive verdict (pressure & wind resistance sensitive; temperature & altitude not) at a clean threshold of COV=1

Validation by applying the same model to three real courses (Tokyo Olympic, Flanders UCI Worlds) plus a self-designed course, and reading off which rider type each course favors (climber vs sprinter) as a sanity check
Closing with a one-page illustrated 'Race Guidance' handout for riders/teams — translating the model into actionable, non-technical advice
why_it_won: "It cleanly satisfied every sub-task of the prompt (rider+gender profiles, three real courses + an original course, two distinct sensitivity analyses, team extension) inside one coherent optimization framework, so the judge sees completeness without disjointedness. The model choices are grounded in cited sport-physiology literature (3P-CP, intensity domains, lactate threshold/VO2max), which signals genuine background research rather than hand-waving. The physics is explicit and segment-by-segment (flat/uphill/downhill/corner force diagrams), making the position-to-power mechanism transparent and checkable. Validation is concrete: real Olympic/UCI courses, numerical per-segment power/speed/time tables, and physically sensible conclusions (steep course favors climber, flat course favors sprinter; uphill needs more power than downhill). The sensitivity work goes beyond a one-variable sweep to a failure-probability ranking with an actionable strategy priority (slope > corner > flat). And the writing is judge-friendly — strong summary, named sub-models, abundant visualizations, and a polished one-page rider handout that demonstrates communication ability prized in MCM."
section_highlights: "Summary front-loads every result with concrete numbers (fit R^2~0.86, course-compliance >90%, plan deviation +-10%, named failure probabilities 0.18%/3.97%/0.18%/4.22%, team strategy 45%/15% splits) so a judge grasps the whole paper in one read. Model section is hierarchically organized as named sub-models (Rider Ability Evaluation, External Factors Analysis, Power Overall Planning) that nest into the final NLP, giving a clear logical spine. Heavy, varied visualization: power-time fit curves, mermaid force-diagram flowcharts for each segment type, radar/hexagon ability charts, course elevation profiles, and combined power/speed/elevation-vs-distance plots. A dedicated Notation table, Assumptions-with-justification section, and explicit segmentation thresholds (slope change <1% => same flat segment; curvature radius <40m => corner) make the model auditable. The standalone illustrated 'Race Guidance' page at the end converts the math into plain rider/team instructions — a distinctive communication flourish."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Many tables and figures are clearly OCR/extraction-garbled in this markdown (e.g. the radar-chart data, several elevation-profile tables, and the W' = (MMP-CP)*t formula appearing inconsistently), and even in the original some numbers look internally inconsistent — e.g. W' = 550 J for a competitive female rider is far too small (real W' is tens of kJ), suggesting a units/scaling error that judges may have overlooked but a referee would flag
The AP and NP formulas are mislabeled/swapped in the noun-explanation table vs the constraint set (the 4th-power normalized form is attributed to AP in one table), an internal inconsistency

The optimization treats per-segment power P_i as free but relies on gradient descent / SLSQP with ad-hoc multi-start looping and no proof or strong evidence of global optimality; 'compare multiple local optima' is asserted, not demonstrated

Validation is weak in the rigorous sense: 'compliance >90%' and 'deviation +-10%' are stated without a clear ground-truth dataset or error metric definition, and there is no comparison against actual rider power files or finish times

The sensitivity 'failure probability' / subset-simulation method is described only in hand-wavy terms; the table mixes COV and 'error rate' as failure probability without a precise definition, and a row x5 appears with no named variable
Bike-control ability BC = height/(2.5*weight) and its link to cornering/downhill speed caps is an unsupported heuristic with no physical or empirical grounding

Team-time-trial parameters a~45%, b~85%, c~130% are reported as solver averages but the drafting model (simply deleting the aerodynamic term for non-leaders) is a crude approximation of peloton aerodynamics
Self-acknowledged weaknesses: too many variables -> high time complexity, and several assumptions hard to realize in practice
reusable_patterns: Two-stage 'characterize the agent, then optimize its behavior on the environment' decomposition: fit a small parameter set describing the actor (here {Pmax, CP, W'}), then feed those parameters as constraints into a physics-based optimization over the environment

Make fit outputs double as optimization constraints — the curve you regress to describe capability becomes the feasibility ceiling (MMP(t)) in the program, avoiding a disconnect between 'analysis' and 'optimization' halves of a paper
Justify every model choice with a citation to domain literature and a reason it fits the relevant regime (e.g. 3P-CP chosen for severe/extreme-domain accuracy) — cheap credibility for judges

Segment a continuous course/process into a small enumerated set of canonical cases (flat/uphill/downhill/corner), each with its own closed-form physics, then sum over segments — turns a continuous control problem into a tractable per-segment NLP
Benchmark several solver backends and report why you picked one (SLSQP for speed); shows engineering judgment cheaply
Provide a 'simplified measurement protocol' fallback for when ideal data is unavailable — demonstrates real-world applicability and pre-empts the 'where does your data come from' objection
Reframe sensitivity analysis as failure-probability ranking across input variables to produce an actionable priority list, rather than a generic +-10% parameter sweep
Validate by re-running the model on multiple real, named, public datasets (Olympic/UCI courses) and checking that qualitative conclusions match domain intuition (course profile -> favored rider type)
Close with a standalone, illustrated one-page guidance/handout aimed at the stakeholder (rider/coach/team) — a high-leverage communication artifact that distinguishes O-award papers
Front-load the Summary with concrete quantitative results for every sub-question so the abstract alone conveys completeness
